description_long:
Palletmind exists because warehouses rarely buy all their automation from one vendor — and every robot brand arrives with its own fleet manager, its own map, and its own queue. The result the site describes is familiar to anyone who has walked a mixed floor: orders stall while one fleet idles and another backs up. Palletmind sells the coordination layer that makes a mixed fleet behave like one system.

Architecturally it sits in the middle: upward integrations to the WMS — Manhattan and Blue Yonder are named — and downward connections to each vendor's fleet manager through its API. The core is a task-allocation solver that decides, every few seconds, which robot should do which job, balancing travel time, battery state, congestion, and order priority across the whole floor, while a shared traffic map prevents deadlocks at intersections and dock doors. It is scheduling and traffic control as a product, abstracted above the hardware.

The digital twin is the adoption wedge. Before go-live, operators replay their busiest day against a proposed layout and see throughput before moving a single rack — an answer to the reasonable fear of handing floor control to new software. The claimed payoff: 20 to 35 percent more picks per hour from the same fleet, and integration in 8 weeks where a custom middleware build typically takes 6 months.

Customers are 3PLs and retailers running mixed fleets across North America and Europe, with annual per-site licensing sized by robot count — pricing that tracks the fleet being orchestrated rather than the throughput it produces.

Founded in Atlanta in 2020 by engineers from an AMR manufacturer, the 61-person company (split across Atlanta and Rotterdam) is betting on a structural trend: as fleets diversify, the vendor-neutral brain becomes the scarce piece. The honest question for any middleware — whether vendors' own tools grow sideways into orchestration — is exactly the race the company's head start on solver and traffic logic is meant to win.
